title: The weekend
canDo: I can ask and talk about what I did at the weekend.

chunks.text: What did you do at the weekend?
chunks.example: Hi! What did you do at the weekend?
I went to the cinema with friends.
It was great — really fun.

chunks.text: Did you have a good time?
chunks.example: Did you have a good time?
I didn't do much, just relaxed.

grammar.pattern: I went · I didn't go · Did you…?

reading.passage: “What did you do at the weekend?” “I went to the cinema with friends. It was great! Did you have a good time?” “Not really — I didn't do much. I stayed home.”

questions.q: Where did the first person go?
Did the second person do a lot?

script.t: What did you do at the weekend?
I went to the cinema. It was great!

fluency432.prompt: Tell me what you did last weekend.

retell.prompt: Retell the two friends' weekend chat.

task.prompt: Ask a friend about their weekend and answer.

writing.model: What did you do at the weekend? I went to the cinema. It was great!

sentenceCombining.answer: I went to the cinema, and it was great.
I stayed home and didn't do much.

task.prompt: Write 2–3 sentences about your last weekend.

quiz.q: You want to ask about someone's weekend.
options: What did you do at the weekend?

quiz.q: Say you didn't do a lot.
options: Did you have a good time?

title: Tonight & plans
canDo: I can talk about plans and invite someone.

chunks.example: What are you doing tonight?

chunks.text: I'm going to visit my family.
chunks.example: I'm going to visit my family this evening.
We're going out — would you like to come?
I'm not sure yet — I'll let you know.
It's been ages — let's catch up soon.

reading.passage: “What are you doing tonight?” “I'm going to visit my family. Would you like to come?” “Maybe — I'll let you know. Let's catch up soon anyway.”

questions.q: What is the person doing tonight?
Did the friend say yes for sure?

script.t: I'm going to visit my family.

dictogloss.sentence: I'm going to visit my family.

fluency432.prompt: Talk about your plans for tonight and the weekend.

task.prompt: Invite a friend to your plans tonight.

writing.model: What are you doing tonight? I'm going to visit my family. Would you like to come?

sentenceCombining.answer: I'm visiting my family — would you like to come?
I'm not sure, so I'll let you know.

task.prompt: Write a message inviting a friend to your plans.

title: Directions
canDo: I can ask for and give simple directions.

chunks.text: How do I get to the station?
chunks.example: Excuse me, how do I get to the station?
Go straight on for two minutes.
The bank? It's on your left.
Is it far from here?

chunks.use: Say something is easy to find.
chunks.example: It's huge — you can't miss it.

reading.passage: “Excuse me, how do I get to the station?” “Go straight on, then it's on your left. It's a big building — you can't miss it.” “Is it far from here?” “No, about five minutes.”

script.t: How do I get to the station?
Go straight on. It's on your left.

fluency432.prompt: Give directions from your home to a shop.

retell.prompt: Retell the directions to the station.

task.prompt: Ask for and give directions to a station.

writing.model: How do I get to the station? Go straight on, then it's on your left. You can't miss it.

sentenceCombining.answer: Go straight on, then turn left at the bank.
It's a big building, so you can't miss it.

task.prompt: Write directions to a place near you (2–3 sentences).

quiz.q: Ask the way to the station.
options: How do I get to the station?

quiz.q: Tell someone the place is easy to find.

title: Shopping & comparing
canDo: I can compare items and ask for alternatives.

chunks.example: This one is cheaper than that one.
Which is better, this or that?
I like it, but it's too expensive.
Have you got anything cheaper?

chunks.text: It's a bit big for me.
chunks.example: It's nice, but it's a bit big for me.

grammar.pattern: cheaper · bigger · more expensive · too + adj

reading.passage: “Which is better?” “This one is cheaper, but the other is nicer.” “It's too expensive. Have you got anything cheaper?” “This one — but it's a bit big for me.”

questions.q: Why doesn't the customer want the first one?
What's the problem with the cheaper one?

script.t: This one is cheaper, but it's smaller.

fluency432.prompt: Compare two things you want to buy.

task.prompt: Compare two products in a shop and choose one.

writing.model: Which is better? This one is cheaper, but it's a bit big for me. That one is too expensive.

sentenceCombining.answer: This one is cheaper, so I'll take it.
It's nice, but it's too expensive.

task.prompt: Compare two things you bought or want (2–3 sentences).

quiz.q: You think the price is too high.

title: Health & advice
canDo: I can say how I feel and give simple advice.

chunks.example: I don't feel well today.
You should see a doctor about that.
I've got a bad headache.
Get some rest and drink water.

chunks.text: I hope you feel better soon.
chunks.example: I hope you feel better soon!

reading.passage: “I don't feel well. I've got a headache.” “You should see a doctor. And get some rest.” “Thanks. I'll take some medicine.” “I hope you feel better soon!”

fluency432.prompt: A friend is ill — give them advice.

retell.prompt: Retell the conversation about feeling ill.

task.prompt: Tell a friend you feel ill; they give advice.

writing.model: I don't feel well — I've got a headache. You should see a doctor and get some rest.

sentenceCombining.answer: I've got a headache and I don't feel well.
You're tired, so you should rest.

task.prompt: Give a sick friend some advice (2–3 sentences) using 'should'.

quiz.q: You feel ill and want to say so.
Advise a friend to see a doctor.

title: Experiences
canDo: I can talk about my experiences using the present perfect.

chunks.text: Have you ever been to London?
chunks.example: Have you ever been to London?
I've never tried sushi before.
I've just finished work.
Have you done your homework yet?
Not yet, but soon.

grammar.pattern: have/has + past participle · ever, never, just, yet

reading.passage: “Have you ever been to London?” “No, never. But I've just booked a trip!” “Have you packed yet?” “Not yet — I'll do it tomorrow. And I've never tried fish and chips!”

questions.q: Has the person been to London before?

script.t: Have you ever been to London?

fluency432.prompt: Talk about places you've been and things you've tried.

retell.prompt: Retell the conversation about the London trip.

task.prompt: Ask a friend about their experiences (places, food).

writing.model: Have you ever been to London? I've never been, but I've just booked a trip. I haven't packed yet.

sentenceCombining.answer: I've booked a trip, but I haven't packed yet.
I've never been to London, but I want to go.

task.prompt: Write 2–3 sentences about your experiences using 'have you ever' / 'I've never'.

quiz.q: Ask if a friend has visited a city.
options: Have you ever been to London?

quiz.q: Say you haven't done something yet.
